content.text: Policy Functions: ARM Template Expressions in Policy Rules

Azure Policy supports a subset of ARM template functions in policy rule conditions and effect details. Functions are called using the same bracket syntax as ARM templates: [functionName(arg1, arg2)]. The available functions depend on the context — the if condition block, the then effect block, and the deployment template inside a DeployIfNotExists effect each support different subsets.

attrs.alt: Azure Policy functions availability by context

content.text: Functions inside a policy rule must be wrapped in double quotes in JSON: "[parameters('effect')]" — not [parameters('effect')]. The brackets are part of the string value, not JSON array syntax.
parameters() — Injecting Assignment-Time Values

The parameters() function is the most commonly used policy function. It reads a value declared in the parameters section of the policy definition and resolved at assignment time. It is available in both the if block and the then block. The primary use cases are making the effect configurable, injecting scope-specific values like a workspace resource ID, and passing allowed value lists.
field() — Reading the Evaluated Resource's Properties in DINE Templates

The field() function reads a property value from the resource currently being evaluated. It is NOT available in the if condition block (use the field key in a condition object instead). It IS available inside the then.details.deployment.parameters section of a DeployIfNotExists effect to pass the evaluated resource's properties as inputs to the ARM deployment template.
resourceGroup() and subscription() — Testing Scope Context

These functions return the metadata object of the resource group or subscription in which the resource lives. They are available in the if block only through the value condition (not via a field key). The most common use is testing the resource group's name or tags, since there is no field alias that exposes parent resource group tags on a child resource.
concat() — Building Dynamic Field Paths

The concat() function joins multiple strings into one. In policy rules, its most common use is building dynamic field paths where the field name itself comes from a parameter. For example, a policy that enforces a required tag can accept the tag name as a parameter and use concat() to build the field path tags['<paramValue>'] dynamically.
toLower() and toUpper() — Case Normalization

Azure Policy field operators like equals and notEquals are case-insensitive for built-in aliases like type and location. However, custom property values (tags, names, SKU strings) may be case-sensitive depending on how they were set. Using toLower() or toUpper() normalizes a value before comparison, preventing case mismatches from causing false Compliant results.
utcNow() — Timestamps in DINE Templates

The utcNow() function returns the current UTC timestamp as a formatted string. It is only available as a default value for a parameter inside a DINE deployment template — it cannot be used in the policyRule if or then blocks. Its primary use is stamping a created-at or remediated-at tag on resources that the DINE deployment creates or modifies.
Function Errors and Debugging
Error / Symptom
Likely Cause
Fix
Policy definition creation fails with 'Invalid function'
Using utcNow() or resourceId() inside the if or then block (not in deployment template)
Move the function call to the DINE deployment template's parameters section
value condition always evaluates to false
resourceGroup() or subscription() used inside a field condition instead of a value condition
Use 'value' key instead of 'field' key for function-based comparisons
concat() result not matching — field path looks correct
Missing escape quotes around the bracket expression in JSON
Ensure the entire expression is a JSON string: "[concat('tags[', parameters('tagName'), ']')]"
parameters('name') returns null at runtime
Parameter not passed in assignment; no defaultValue defined
Add a defaultValue to the parameter declaration, or ensure the assignment passes the value
field() value is empty in DINE template
The alias path inside field() does not match the resource type or the property is not set on the resource
Use a conditional check (if property exists) in the template before using the field value

# Debug a policy function by testing it against a known resource

# Step 1: Create a test policy with Audit effect that echoes the function result
# (policy will log the evaluated value as part of the compliance reason code)
az policy definition create \
  --name debug-policy-fn \
  --mode All \
  --rules '{
    "if": {
      "value": "[resourceGroup().name]",
      "equals": "__never_match__"
    },
    "then": { "effect": "audit" }
  }'

# Step 2: Assign it at resource group scope and trigger a scan
az policy assignment create --name debug-fn-test --policy debug-policy-fn --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-test
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription $SUB_ID

# Step 3: Check the compliance state reason code — it often includes the evaluated value
az policy state list \
  --subscription $SUB_ID \
  --filter "policyAssignmentName eq 'debug-fn-test'" \
  --query '[0].reasons' -o json

# Step 4: Clean up
az policy assignment delete --name debug-fn-test --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-test
az policy definition delete --name debug-policy-fn
Summary

Azure Policy functions use ARM template expression syntax ([functionName(args)]) and are available in specific contexts. parameters() works everywhere — it is the standard way to make policies configurable at assignment time. field() is only valid inside a DINE deployment template's parameter inputs, not in if conditions (use the field condition object instead). resourceGroup() and subscription() provide scope context in value conditions. concat() builds dynamic field paths from parameter values. toLower() and toUpper() normalize values for case-insensitive comparisons. utcNow() is restricted to deployment template parameter defaultValues only. Using a function in the wrong context produces a silent evaluation error rather than a validation failure, making context awareness critical when authoring complex policies.
